description: The porbeagle is a large, cold-water shark in the family Lamnidae, closely related to the great white and mako sharks. Found in cold and temperate waters of the North and South Atlantic and the Southern Ocean, it is one of the few sharks capable of maintaining body temperature above ambient water temperature. Porbeagles were once heavily targeted for meat and fins, causing steep population declines in the North Atlantic.
funFact: Like great whites and makos, the porbeagle is warm-blooded — it can maintain its body temperature 8–10°C above surrounding cold water, giving it a significant speed and metabolic advantage when hunting in frigid, high-latitude seas.
habitat: Cold to temperate oceanic and coastal waters. Found in the North Atlantic from Norway to the Mediterranean, and in the Southern Hemisphere around southern South America, South Africa, and Australia. Occupies depths from the surface to over 700 m.
diet: Feeds primarily on schooling bony fish — particularly mackerel, herring, and sand lance — as well as squid and octopus. Uses its warm-bloodedness to hunt actively and efficiently in cold water where most other large predators are metabolically limited.
size: Typically 1.5–2.5 m (5–8 ft); max confirmed ~3.5 m (11.5 ft). Females are larger than males.

vitalSigns.label: Estimated Population
vitalSigns.value: Unknown — significantly depleted in the North Atlantic

vitalSigns.label: Diet
vitalSigns.value: Mackerel, herring, sand lance, squid, and octopus

vitalSigns.label: Population Growth Rate
vitalSigns.value: Low — North Atlantic populations showing early signs of recovery under protection

vitalSigns.label: Key Adaptations
vitalSigns.value: Counter-current heat exchange (endothermy) maintains body temperature 8–10°C above ambient seawater; specialized for cold-water, high-latitude hunting

threats.name: Targeted Commercial Fishing
threats.description: Historically the target of intense directed fisheries in the North Atlantic — Norwegian, Danish, and UK fleets — primarily for high-quality meat sold in European markets. Northeast Atlantic populations collapsed by the 1960s–70s.

threats.name: Bycatch
threats.description: Caught as bycatch in pelagic longlines, bottom trawls, and gillnets throughout its range. High-grading and discarding obscure true mortality in official landing statistics.

threats.name: Finning
threats.description: Fins are valued in the shark fin trade. Despite EU finning bans, enforcement gaps allow some illegal finning to continue, particularly in high-seas fisheries outside jurisdictional reach.

threats.name: Slow Reproductive Rate
threats.description: Small litters and multi-year maturity timelines limit how quickly populations can rebound. Even under reduced fishing pressure, recovery to historical levels takes decades.

threats.name: Climate Change
threats.description: A cold-water specialist that depends on productive high-latitude fisheries. Ocean warming may shift prey distributions poleward, compressing available habitat and reducing prey density.

actionItems.title: Support Northeast Atlantic Recovery
actionItems.description: The Northeast Atlantic porbeagle population has shown early signs of recovery under EU protection. Advocate for continued fishing prohibitions and oppose any proposals to reopen directed fisheries.

actionItems.title: Avoid Shark Meat Products
actionItems.description: Porbeagle is sold under names like 'flake', 'dogfish', or 'rock salmon' in European fish markets. Use sustainable seafood guides to identify and avoid purchasing shark species.

actionItems.title: Support CITES Trade Controls
actionItems.description: The porbeagle is listed on CITES Appendix II. Advocate for rigorous enforcement of trade documentation requirements to prevent unreported imports and exports of porbeagle products.

actionItems.title: Donate to Shark Conservation
actionItems.description: Support the Shark Trust and similar organizations that monitor porbeagle bycatch, advocate for RFMO protections, and fund research into population status across the Southern Hemisphere.

healthMetrics.label: IUCN Status
healthMetrics.value: Vulnerable (globally); Critically Endangered in Northeast Atlantic

healthMetrics.label: Population Trend
healthMetrics.value: Recovering in Northeast Atlantic; depleted elsewhere

healthMetrics.label: Habitat Quality
healthMetrics.value: Moderate — cold-water habitats intact but shifting poleward with warming

healthMetrics.label: Prey Availability
healthMetrics.value: Declining — mackerel and herring stocks under heavy pressure in North Atlantic

healthMetrics.label: Fishing Pressure
healthMetrics.value: High — bycatch continues globally; directed fisheries in some regions

healthMetrics.label: Protection Coverage
healthMetrics.value: Partial — CITES Appendix II; EU directed fishery ban; North Atlantic recovery underway
